Exploring Genomics of Drug Sensitivity in Cancer (GDSC)¶

Overview:¶

In this project, you will explore a real-world dataset on drug sensitivity in cancer, focusing on how genomic features influence response to treatment. Please note that the goal is not to arrive at a single correct answer, but to investigate patterns in the data, ask meaningful and contextual biological questions, demonstrate various data handling and cleaning techniques and of course, communicate results clearly.

Try as much as possible to be innovative, creative and use clear graphics to showcase your results clearly.

Metadata¶

Columns: Description

COSMIC_ID: Unique Identifier on COSMIC

CELL_LINE_NAME: Name of cell line

TCGA_DESC: TCGA Code for cancer type

DRUG_ID: Drug ID

DRUG_NAME: Drug Name

LN_IC50: Natural logarithm of the IC50

AUC: Area under the dose dependent curve

Z_SCORE: Z score of deviations from the mean of zero; the closer to zero, the better

GDSC Tissue descriptor 1: System description

GDSC Tissue descriptor 2: Specific organ or tissue

Cancer Type (matching TCGA label): Same as the TCGA

Microsatellite instability Status (MSI): MSI-L (Minor instability), MSI-H; High instability, MSS: No instability or repaired mutation

Screen Medium: Medium for screening

Growth Properties: Growth properties

CNA: Is copy number altered?

Gene Expression: Is gene expressed?

Methylation: Is gene methylated?

TARGET: genetic target of drug

TARGET_PATHWAY: pathway of genetic target

In [1]:
import pandas as pd
!pip install openpyxl
gdsc = pd.read_excel('https://github.com/HackBio-Internship/public_datasets/raw/refs/heads/main/GDSC.xlsx')

Requirement already satisfied: openpyxl in /usr/local/lib/python3.12/dist-packages (3.1.5)
Requirement already satisfied: et-xmlfile in /usr/local/lib/python3.12/dist-packages (from openpyxl) (2.0.0)

Visualizing Distributions of Numerical Features¶

Let's visualize the distributions of LN_IC50, AUC, and Z_SCORE using histograms to understand their spread, central tendency, and any potential skewness or outliers.

In [8]:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns

# Set the aesthetic style of the plots
sns.set_style("whitegrid")

plt.figure(figsize=(18, 5))

# Histogram for LN_IC50
plt.subplot(1, 3, 1) # 1 row, 3 columns, 1st plot
sns.histplot(gdsc['LN_IC50'], kde=True, bins=50, color='skyblue')
plt.title('Distribution of LN_IC50')
plt.xlabel('LN_IC50 (Natural Log of IC50)')
plt.ylabel('Frequency')

# Histogram for AUC
plt.subplot(1, 3, 2) # 1 row, 3 columns, 2nd plot
sns.histplot(gdsc['AUC'], kde=True, bins=50, color='lightcoral')
plt.title('Distribution of AUC')
plt.xlabel('AUC (Area Under Curve)')
plt.ylabel('Frequency')

# Histogram for Z_SCORE
plt.subplot(1, 3, 3) # 1 row, 3 columns, 3rd plot
sns.histplot(gdsc['Z_SCORE'], kde=True, bins=50, color='lightgreen')
plt.title('Distribution of Z_SCORE')
plt.xlabel('Z_SCORE')
plt.ylabel('Frequency')

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Comments

Distribution of LN_IC50 (Natural Log of IC50):

While many drug-cell line combinations show a moderate LN_IC50, there's a tendency for a larger number of cases to have lower (more potent) IC50 values.

Negative LN_IC50 values indicate extremely potent drugs, as they achieve half-maximal inhibition at very low concentrations. The more negative the LN_IC50, the more potent the drug.

The tail extending to the right suggests some instances of very high IC50 values, meaning low drug potency.

Distribution of AUC (Area Under Curve):

The AUC histogram is heavily skewed towards higher values (closer to 1) indicating that a significant portion of drug-cell line interactions results in relatively high resistance.

A high AUC means the drug is less effective over the dose range tested.

The smaller presence of values closer to 0 indicates cases where the drug is more effective or where the cells are more sensitive to the drug.

Distribution of Z_SCORE:

The Z_SCORE distribution is roughly centered around zero, thus, indicating that a large number of drug-cell line interactions result in responses that are close to the average effectiveness for that particular drug.

Values far from zero (both positive and negative) represent responses that deviate significantly from the average for that drug.

Negative Z-scores indicate increased sensitivity, while positive Z-scores indicate increased resistance.

Exploring Value Counts for Categorical Features¶

Let's examine the unique values and their frequencies for each categorical column to understand the distribution within these features.

In [9]:
import os

for column in gdsc.select_dtypes(include='object').columns:
    print(f"\nValue Counts for '{column}':")
    value_counts = gdsc[column].value_counts()

    # Set a threshold for when to save to CSV vs. display directly
    if len(value_counts) > 500: # For example, if more than 500 unique values
        file_name = f"{column}_value_counts.csv"
        value_counts.to_csv(file_name)
        print(f"  (Full value counts for {column} saved to '{file_name}' due to large number of unique values ({len(value_counts)}). Showing top 10 below:)")
        display(value_counts.head(10))
    else:
        display(value_counts)
    print("--------------------------------------------------")

Correlation Analysis of Numerical Features¶

Let's examine the correlation matrix for the numerical features (LN_IC50, AUC, Z_SCORE, COSMIC_ID, DRUG_ID) to understand the relationships between them. A correlation matrix helps to identify how strongly and in what direction two variables are related.

In [10]:
# Select only numerical columns for correlation calculation
numerical_cols = gdsc.select_dtypes(include=['number'])

# Calculate the correlation matrix
correlation_matrix = numerical_cols.corr()

# Display the correlation matrix
print("Correlation Matrix of Numerical Features:")
display(correlation_matrix)

Correlation Matrix of Numerical Features:
COSMIC_ID DRUG_ID LN_IC50 AUC Z_SCORE
COSMIC_ID 1.000000 -0.005969 -0.019769 -0.011371 -0.038508
DRUG_ID -0.005969 1.000000 0.172981 0.049615 -0.000016
LN_IC50 -0.019769 0.172981 1.000000 0.760447 0.501577
AUC -0.011371 0.049615 0.760447 1.000000 0.443160
Z_SCORE -0.038508 -0.000016 0.501577 0.443160 1.000000

Comments on Correlation Matrix

  • LN_IC50 and AUC: These two metrics, representing drug sensitivity, show a strong positive correlation. This is expected, as both generally increase with decreasing drug efficacy. A higher LN_IC50 (meaning higher IC50) and higher AUC both indicate greater resistance.
  • LN_IC50 and Z_SCORE: These also show a strong positive correlation, which is consistent. A higher LN_IC50 suggests less sensitivity, and a higher Z_SCORE also indicates greater resistance (deviation towards resistance from the mean).
  • AUC and Z_SCORE: Similarly, these exhibit a strong positive correlation, reinforcing the understanding that higher AUC values (less effective drug) correspond to higher Z_SCOREs (more resistant response).
  • COSMIC_ID and DRUG_ID: These are identifiers and are not expected to have meaningful correlations with drug sensitivity metrics or with each other, as observed by their near-zero correlations. Their correlations with other features are also negligible, which is appropriate for identifiers.
In [11]:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns

# Set up the matplotlib figure
plt.figure(figsize=(8, 6))

# Draw the heatmap with the mask and correct aspect ratio
sns.heatmap(correlation_matrix, annot=True, cmap='coolwarm', fmt=".2f", linewidths=.5)
plt.title('Correlation Matrix of Numerical Features')
plt.show()

Comments

In essence, the heatmap confirms that LN_IC50, AUC, and Z_SCORE are all related and generally move in the same direction when indicating drug sensitivity or resistance, with LN_IC50 and AUC showing the strongest interdependence.

Pairplot of Numerical Features¶

Let's generate a pairplot to visualize the pairwise relationships and distributions of LN_IC50, AUC, and Z_SCORE. This helps in understanding correlations, identifying patterns, and detecting outliers.

In [12]:
import seaborn as sns
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Select the relevant numerical columns for the pairplot
relevant_cols = ['LN_IC50', 'AUC', 'Z_SCORE']

# Create the pairplot
fig = sns.pairplot(gdsc[relevant_cols], kind='reg', plot_kws={'line_kws':{'color':'red'}})
fig.fig.suptitle('Pairplot of LN_IC50, AUC, and Z_SCORE', y=1.02) # Add a title to the figure
plt.show()

Comments

The pairplot visually confirms and elaborates on the relationships between LN_IC50, AUC, and Z_SCORE.

Diagonal Plots (Histograms): These display the distribution of each variable individually.

LN_IC50: Shows a slightly left-skewed distribution, indicating more potent responses are common, but with a tail towards higher (less potent) values.

AUC: Heavily skewed towards higher values, suggesting a prevalence of less effective drug interactions.

Z_SCORE: Centered around zero, with values deviating in both positive (resistance) and negative (sensitivity) directions.

Off-Diagonal Plots (Scatter Plots with Regression Lines): These plots show the pairwise relationships between the variables:

LN_IC50 vs. AUC: The scatter plot and the red regression line clearly show a strong positive linear relationship. As LN_IC50 increases (less sensitive), AUC also tends to increase (less effective), which is expected.

LN_IC50 vs. Z_SCORE: A strong positive linear correlation is also visible here. Higher LN_IC50 values correspond to higher Z_SCORE values, indicating increased resistance.

AUC vs. Z_SCORE: Similarly, there's a strong positive linear relationship. As AUC increases (less effective), Z_SCORE also tends to increase (more resistant response).

Overall, the pairplot provides a clear visual representation of the strong positive correlations previously identified in the correlation matrix, highlighting that these three metrics move together in indicating drug sensitivity or resistance.

Scatter Plot: LN_IC50 vs. AUC for All Drugs¶

Let's visualize the relationship between LN_IC50 and AUC across all drugs using a scatter plot. This plot will help us understand the distribution and the confirmed strong positive correlation between these two key drug effectiveness metrics more broadly.

In [13]:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns

sns.set_style("whitegrid")

plt.figure(figsize=(10, 7))
sns.scatterplot(x='LN_IC50', y='AUC', data=gdsc, alpha=0.6)
plt.title('Scatter Plot of LN_IC50 vs. AUC for All Drugs')
plt.xlabel('LN_IC50 (Natural Log of IC50)')
plt.ylabel('AUC (Area Under Curve)')
plt.grid(True, linestyle='--', alpha=0.7)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Comments

The scatter plot visually confirms the strong positive correlation between LN_IC50 and AUC that we observed in the correlation matrix and the pairplot.

As LN_IC50 increases (indicating lower potency), AUC also tends to increase (indicating lower overall effectiveness).

The spread of the points illustrates the variability in this relationship across different drug-cell line combinations, but the general upward trend is evident.

This plot provides a concise and intuitive way to understand how these two key drug effectiveness metrics move together.

In [14]:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns

sns.set_style("whitegrid")

plt.figure(figsize=(10, 7))
sns.scatterplot(x='LN_IC50', y='Z_SCORE', data=gdsc, alpha=0.6)
plt.title('Scatter Plot of LN_IC50 vs. Z_SCORE for All Drugs')
plt.xlabel('LN_IC50 (Natural Log of IC50)')
plt.ylabel('Z_SCORE')
plt.grid(True, linestyle='--', alpha=0.7)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Comments

As LN_IC50 increases (indicating lower potency), Z_SCORE also tends to increase (indicating higher resistance).

This confirms that both metrics are generally in agreement regarding drug effectiveness across the dataset.

In [15]:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns

sns.set_style("whitegrid")

plt.figure(figsize=(10, 7))
sns.regplot(x='AUC', y='Z_SCORE', data=gdsc, scatter_kws={'alpha':0.6}, line_kws={'color':'red'})
plt.title('Scatter Plot of AUC vs. Z_SCORE for All Drugs with Regression Line')
plt.xlabel('AUC (Area Under Curve)')
plt.ylabel('Z_SCORE')
plt.grid(True, linestyle='--', alpha=0.7)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Comments

This regression plot visually confirms the strong positive correlation between AUC and Z_SCORE.

The upward sloping red regression line indicates that as AUC increases (drugs become less effective), the Z_SCORE also tends to increase (indicating higher resistance).

This reinforces the understanding that both metrics are consistent indicators of drug effectiveness.

Drug Sensitivity patterns¶

Identifying Most Effective Drugs¶

To determine which drugs appear to be most effective, we will analyze the average values of LN_IC50, AUC, and Z_SCORE for each drug. Lower LN_IC50 and AUC values, and more negative Z_SCORE values, indicate greater drug effectiveness.

In [16]:
import numpy as np

# Group by DRUG_NAME and calculate the mean for the sensitivity metrics
drug_effectiveness = gdsc.groupby('DRUG_NAME')[['LN_IC50', 'AUC', 'Z_SCORE']].mean().reset_index()

print("Top 10 Drugs by Lowest Average LN_IC50 (most potent):")
display(drug_effectiveness.sort_values(by='LN_IC50').head(10))

print("\nTop 10 Drugs by Lowest Average AUC (most effective):")
display(drug_effectiveness.sort_values(by='AUC').head(10))

print("\nTop 10 Drugs by Most Negative Average Z_SCORE (highest sensitivity):")
display(drug_effectiveness.sort_values(by='Z_SCORE').head(10))

Top 10 Drugs by Lowest Average LN_IC50 (most potent):
DRUG_NAME LN_IC50 AUC Z_SCORE
185 Romidepsin -5.177964 0.803534 0.060958
45 Bortezomib -4.746884 0.779567 -0.008408
200 Sepantronium bromide -4.056148 0.205525 0.063142

195 SN-38 -3.359790 0.769116 0.032973
231 Vinblastine -3.353560 0.718984 0.063531

67 Daporinad -3.322740 0.638247 0.022480
173 Paclitaxel -3.005582 0.874649 0.050876
77 Eg5_9814 -2.886607 0.777225 0.025662 
Top 10 Drugs by Lowest Average AUC (most effective):
DRUG_NAME LN_IC50 AUC Z_SCORE
200 Sepantronium bromide -4.056148 0.205525 0.063142
203 Staurosporine -2.753419 0.406821 0.028684
51 CDK9_5038 -2.017681 0.517699 0.058003
136 MG-132 -1.288915 0.526065 -0.007645
69 Dinaciclib -2.609900 0.582044 0.028122
196 Sabutoclax -0.263076 0.595061 0.065111
52 CDK9_5576 -0.132813 0.600394 0.037025
65 Dactinomycin -3.670432 0.627695 0.062702
67 Daporinad -3.322740 0.638247 0.022480
105 Gemcitabine -0.631929 0.659869 0.044488 
Top 10 Drugs by Most Negative Average Z_SCORE (highest sensitivity):
DRUG_NAME LN_IC50 AUC Z_SCORE
183 Refametinib 2.127847 0.812440 -0.076374
165 PD0325901 1.206118 0.867602 -0.063512
199 Selumetinib 3.459949 0.892482 -0.062325
218 Trametinib 0.089114 0.818366 -0.056452
21 AZD6482 3.316181 0.907171 -0.055349
191 SCH772984 2.371954 0.873866 -0.045804
197 Sapitinib 3.636424 0.917213 -0.042888
76 ERK_6604 3.245353 0.891326 -0.028366
228 VX-11e 2.671350 0.864831 -0.027217
75 ERK_2440 2.523962 0.848196 -0.025504

These tables show the drugs that consistently rank high in effectiveness based on the different sensitivity metrics. Drugs appearing in the top ranks across all three metrics are likely the most broadly effective.

Visualizing Top 10 Most Potent Drugs by LN_IC50¶

Let's visualize the top 10 drugs with the lowest average LN_IC50 values, indicating they are the most potent. A bar plot will effectively highlight these drugs and their relative potencies.

In [17]:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns

sns.set_style("whitegrid")

# Get the top 10 drugs by lowest average LN_IC50 (most potent)
most_potent_drugs = drug_effectiveness.sort_values(by='LN_IC50', ascending=True).head(10)

plt.figure(figsize=(12, 7))
sns.barplot(x='LN_IC50', y='DRUG_NAME', data=most_potent_drugs, hue='DRUG_NAME', palette='viridis', legend=False)
plt.title('Top 10 Most Potent Drugs by Average LN_IC50 (Lower is More Potent)')
plt.xlabel('Average LN_IC50')
plt.ylabel('Drug Name')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Comments

The most potent drug (with the lowest average LN_IC50) is Romidepsin.

Among these top 10 drugs, the least potent drug (with the highest average LN_IC50 in this selection) is Eg5_9814.

Visualizing AUC Scores for Top 10 Most Potent Drugs¶

Let's compare the AUC scores for the same top 10 drugs identified as most potent by LN_IC50. A lower AUC value indicates greater overall effectiveness.

In [18]:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns

sns.set_style("whitegrid")

# Sort the most potent drugs by AUC (lower is better) for this specific plot
most_potent_drugs_by_auc = most_potent_drugs.sort_values(by='AUC', ascending=True)

plt.figure(figsize=(12, 7))
sns.barplot(x='AUC', y='DRUG_NAME', data=most_potent_drugs_by_auc, hue='DRUG_NAME', palette='magma', legend=False)
plt.title('AUC Scores for Top 10 Most Potent Drugs (Lower is More Effective)')
plt.xlabel('Average AUC')
plt.ylabel('Drug Name')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Comments

Among these highly potent drugs, 'Sepantronium bromide' stands out with a significantly lower average AUC, indicating it is not only potent but also highly effective overall.

Other drugs like 'Dactinomycin' and 'Daporinad' also show relatively good AUC scores, suggesting good overall effectiveness, although higher than Sepantronium bromide.

This visualization helps to further refine our understanding of these drugs' performance by considering another key metric.

Visualizing Top 10 Most Potent Drugs by Z_SCORE¶

Let's visualize the top 10 drugs with the most negative average Z_SCORE values, indicating they are the most sensitive/potent. A bar plot will effectively highlight these drugs and their relative Z_SCOREs.

In [19]:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns

sns.set_style("whitegrid")

# Get the top 10 drugs by most negative average Z_SCORE (highest sensitivity)
most_sensitive_drugs = drug_effectiveness.sort_values(by='Z_SCORE', ascending=True).head(10)

plt.figure(figsize=(12, 7))
sns.barplot(x='Z_SCORE', y='DRUG_NAME', data=most_sensitive_drugs, hue='DRUG_NAME', palette='cividis', legend=False)
plt.title('Top 10 Most Potent Drugs by Average Z_SCORE (More Negative is More Potent)')
plt.xlabel('Average Z_SCORE')
plt.ylabel('Drug Name')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Comments

This plot visually identifies the drugs that induce the highest sensitivity (most negative Z_SCORE) in cell lines.

By comparing this to the LN_IC50 and AUC plots, we can see if there is consistency across all three metrics for identifying the most potent drugs.

Identifying Overlapping Effective Drugs¶

To find drugs that are consistently effective across all three metrics (LN_IC50, AUC, and Z_SCORE), we'll identify the drugs that appear in the top 10 for each of these metrics and then find their intersection.

In [20]:
# Get the top 10 drugs for each metric
top_10_ln_ic50 = set(drug_effectiveness.sort_values(by='LN_IC50').head(10)['DRUG_NAME'])
top_10_auc = set(drug_effectiveness.sort_values(by='AUC').head(10)['DRUG_NAME'])
top_10_z_score = set(drug_effectiveness.sort_values(by='Z_SCORE').head(10)['DRUG_NAME'])

# Find the intersection of these sets
overlapping_effective_drugs = top_10_ln_ic50.intersection(top_10_auc, top_10_z_score)

print("Drugs appearing in the top 10 across all three effectiveness metrics:")
if overlapping_effective_drugs:
    for drug in overlapping_effective_drugs:
        print(f"- {drug}")
else:
    print("No drugs found that are in the top 10 for all three metrics.")

Drugs appearing in the top 10 across all three effectiveness metrics:
No drugs found that are in the top 10 for all three metrics.

Identifying Drugs Effective Across At Least Two Metrics¶

Since no drug appeared in the top 10 for all three metrics, let's broaden our search to identify drugs that are consistently effective across at least two of the three metrics: LN_IC50, AUC, and Z_SCORE. This will help highlight drugs with broader effectiveness profiles.

In [21]:
import collections

# Calculate intersections for each pair of metrics
overlap_ln_ic50_auc = top_10_ln_ic50.intersection(top_10_auc)
overlap_ln_ic50_z_score = top_10_ln_ic50.intersection(top_10_z_score)
overlap_auc_z_score = top_10_auc.intersection(top_10_z_score)

# Combine all drugs that appear in at least two metrics using a union
drugs_in_at_least_two_metrics = overlap_ln_ic50_auc.union(overlap_ln_ic50_z_score, overlap_auc_z_score)

print("Drugs appearing in the top 10 for at least two effectiveness metrics:")
if drugs_in_at_least_two_metrics:
    # Create a dictionary to store which metrics each drug is in the top 10 for
    drug_metric_breakdown = collections.defaultdict(list)
    for drug in drugs_in_at_least_two_metrics:
        if drug in top_10_ln_ic50:
            drug_metric_breakdown[drug].append('LN_IC50')
        if drug in top_10_auc:
            drug_metric_breakdown[drug].append('AUC')
        if drug in top_10_z_score:
            drug_metric_breakdown[drug].append('Z_SCORE')

    # Print the drugs along with their corresponding metrics
    for drug in sorted(drug_metric_breakdown.keys()):
        metrics = ', '.join(drug_metric_breakdown[drug])
        print(f"- {drug} ({metrics})")
else:
    print("No drugs found that are in the top 10 for at least two metrics.")

Drugs appearing in the top 10 for at least two effectiveness metrics:
- Dactinomycin (LN_IC50, AUC)
- Daporinad (LN_IC50, AUC)
- Sepantronium bromide (LN_IC50, AUC)

Comments

Based on the analysis, the following drugs appear in the top 10 for at least two (LN_IC50, and AUC)of the three effectiveness metrics:

  1. Dactinomycin

  2. Daporinad

  3. Sepantronium bromide

This indicates that these three drugs consistently demonstrate high potency (low LN_IC50) and overall effectiveness (low AUC) across various cell lines, making them strong candidates for further investigation as broadly effective agents.

Visualizing Effectiveness of Top Drugs¶

To further understand the effectiveness profiles of 'Dactinomycin', 'Daporinad', and 'Sepantronium bromide', we will visualize their distributions across LN_IC50, AUC, and Z_SCORE. This will help us compare their spread and central tendency for each metric.

In [22]:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns

# Filter the original DataFrame for the identified effective drugs
effective_drugs_list = ['Dactinomycin', 'Daporinad', 'Sepantronium bromide']
df_effective = gdsc[gdsc['DRUG_NAME'].isin(effective_drugs_list)]

sns.set_style("whitegrid")

plt.figure(figsize=(18, 6))

# Box plot for LN_IC50
plt.subplot(1, 3, 1)
sns.boxplot(x='DRUG_NAME', y='LN_IC50', data=df_effective, hue='DRUG_NAME', palette='viridis', legend=False)
plt.title('LN_IC50 Distribution for Effective Drugs')
plt.xlabel('Drug Name')
plt.ylabel('LN_IC50')
plt.xticks(rotation=45, ha='right')

# Box plot for AUC
plt.subplot(1, 3, 2)
sns.boxplot(x='DRUG_NAME', y='AUC', data=df_effective, hue='DRUG_NAME', palette='magma', legend=False)
plt.title('AUC Distribution for Effective Drugs')
plt.xlabel('Drug Name')
plt.ylabel('AUC')
plt.xticks(rotation=45, ha='right')

# Box plot for Z_SCORE
plt.subplot(1, 3, 3)
sns.boxplot(x='DRUG_NAME', y='Z_SCORE', data=df_effective, hue='DRUG_NAME', palette='cividis', legend=False)
plt.title('Z_SCORE Distribution for Effective Drugs')
plt.xlabel('Drug Name')
plt.ylabel('Z_SCORE')
plt.xticks(rotation=45, ha='right')

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

These box plots illustrate the distribution of LN_IC50, AUC, and Z_SCORE for each of the three identified drugs.

  • LN_IC50 Distribution: Lower values indicate higher potency. Sepantronium bromide shows a distribution with generally lower (more negative) LN_IC50 values, suggesting it is highly potent.

  • AUC Distribution: Lower values indicate greater overall effectiveness. Sepantronium bromide again stands out with its AUC distribution heavily skewed towards lower values, reinforcing its strong effectiveness.

  • Z_SCORE Distribution: More negative values indicate higher sensitivity. Sepantronium bromide's Z_SCORE distribution is centered around slightly positive values, indicating a mixed response but still appearing in the top metrics due to its strong performance in AUC and LN_IC50.

Statistical Significance Test: ANOVA and Post-Hoc Analysis¶

To formally compare the effectiveness of 'Dactinomycin', 'Daporinad', and 'Sepantronium bromide', we will use a One-Way Analysis of Variance (ANOVA). ANOVA tests if there are significant differences between the means of three or more independent groups. If the ANOVA test yields a significant p-value (typically p < 0.05), it indicates that at least one drug's effectiveness is significantly different from another's.

Following a significant ANOVA result, we will perform a Tukey's Honestly Significant Difference (HSD) post-hoc test. This test allows us to make pairwise comparisons between the drug groups while controlling for the family-wise error rate, determining exactly which pairs of drugs have statistically significant differences in their effectiveness metrics.

In [23]:
import scipy.stats as stats
from statsmodels.stats.multicomp import pairwise_tukeyhsd
import pandas as pd

# Filter the original DataFrame for the identified effective drugs
effective_drugs_list = ['Dactinomycin', 'Daporinad', 'Sepantronium bromide']
df_effective = gdsc[gdsc['DRUG_NAME'].isin(effective_drugs_list)].copy()

metrics = ['LN_IC50', 'AUC', 'Z_SCORE']

for metric in metrics:
    print(f"\n--- ANOVA for {metric} ---")
    # Perform one-way ANOVA
    f_statistic, p_value = stats.f_oneway(
        df_effective[df_effective['DRUG_NAME'] == 'Dactinomycin'][metric],
        df_effective[df_effective['DRUG_NAME'] == 'Daporinad'][metric],
        df_effective[df_effective['DRUG_NAME'] == 'Sepantronium bromide'][metric]
    )

    print(f"F-statistic: {f_statistic:.3f}")
    print(f"P-value: {p_value:.3f}")

    if p_value < 0.05:
        print(f"Significant difference found for {metric} (p < 0.05). Performing Tukey's HSD post-hoc test.")

        # Perform Tukey's HSD post-hoc test
        tukey_result = pairwise_tukeyhsd(
            endog=df_effective[metric],
            groups=df_effective['DRUG_NAME'],
            alpha=0.05
        )
        print(tukey_result)
    else:
        print(f"No significant difference found for {metric} (p >= 0.05).")

--- ANOVA for LN_IC50 ---
F-statistic: 19.577
P-value: 0.000
Significant difference found for LN_IC50 (p < 0.05). Performing Tukey's HSD post-hoc test.
          Multiple Comparison of Means - Tukey HSD, FWER=0.05           
========================================================================
   group1           group2        meandiff p-adj   lower   upper  reject
------------------------------------------------------------------------
Dactinomycin            Daporinad   0.3477 0.0045  0.0901  0.6053   True
Dactinomycin Sepantronium bromide  -0.3857 0.0001 -0.6032 -0.1682   True
   Daporinad Sepantronium bromide  -0.7334    0.0 -1.0147 -0.4521   True
------------------------------------------------------------------------

--- ANOVA for AUC ---
F-statistic: 677.300
P-value: 0.000
Significant difference found for AUC (p < 0.05). Performing Tukey's HSD post-hoc test.
          Multiple Comparison of Means - Tukey HSD, FWER=0.05           
========================================================================
   group1           group2        meandiff p-adj   lower   upper  reject
------------------------------------------------------------------------
Dactinomycin            Daporinad   0.0106 0.7433 -0.0232  0.0443  False
Dactinomycin Sepantronium bromide  -0.4222    0.0 -0.4506 -0.3937   True
   Daporinad Sepantronium bromide  -0.4327    0.0 -0.4695 -0.3959   True
------------------------------------------------------------------------

--- ANOVA for Z_SCORE ---
F-statistic: 0.298
P-value: 0.742
No significant difference found for Z_SCORE (p >= 0.05).

Interpretation of Statistical Tests¶

ANOVA Results:

  • For each metric (LN_IC50, AUC, Z_SCORE), if the P-value is less than 0.05, we reject the null hypothesis, meaning there is a statistically significant difference in the average effectiveness of at least one pair of drugs.
  • If the P-value is greater than or equal to 0.05, we do not have sufficient evidence to claim a significant difference in average effectiveness among the drugs for that metric.

Tukey's HSD Post-Hoc Test (if ANOVA is significant):

  • The Tukey's HSD table shows pairwise comparisons between the drug names.
  • Look at the p-adj (adjusted p-value) column for each comparison:
    • If p-adj is less than 0.05, the difference between that pair of drugs is considered statistically significant.
    • If p-adj is greater than or equal to 0.05, there is no statistically significant difference between that pair of drugs.
  • The meandiff column indicates the difference in means between the two groups being compared. A positive value means the mean of the 'group2' is higher than 'group1', and a negative value means it's lower.
  • lower and upper provide the confidence interval for the mean difference.

Comments

For LN_IC50 (Potency): All three drugs are significantly different from each other in terms of potency. 'Sepantronium bromide' is the most potent, followed by 'Dactinomycin', and then 'Daporinad'.

For AUC (Overall Effectiveness): Conclusion for AUC: 'Sepantronium bromide' is significantly more effective overall than both 'Dactinomycin' and 'Daporinad'. However, 'Dactinomycin' and 'Daporinad' do not show a statistically significant difference in their overall effectiveness.

For Z_SCORE (Sensitivity): ANOVA Result: The P-value is 0.742, which is greater than 0.05. This means there is no statistically significant difference in the average Z_SCORE among 'Dactinomycin', 'Daporinad', and 'Sepantronium bromide'. Their average sensitivities are statistically similar.

Overall Summary:

'Sepantronium bromide' stands out as having significantly higher potency (LN_IC50) and greater overall effectiveness (AUC) compared to both 'Dactinomycin' and 'Daporinad'.

While 'Dactinomycin' is more potent than 'Daporinad', their overall effectiveness (AUC) is similar.

For general sensitivity (Z_SCORE), there's no statistically significant difference identified among these three drugs.

Identifying Least Effective Drugs Overall¶

To identify the drug with the least effectiveness across the entire dataset, we will analyze drugs with the highest average LN_IC50, highest average AUC, and highest average Z_SCORE.

Visualizing Top 10 Least Potent Drugs by LN_IC50

Let's visualize the top 10 drugs with the highest average LN_IC50 values, indicating they are the least potent. A bar plot will effectively highlight these drugs and their relative potencies.

In [24]:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns

sns.set_style("whitegrid")

# Get the top 10 drugs by highest average LN_IC50 (least potent)
least_potent_drugs = drug_effectiveness.sort_values(by='LN_IC50', ascending=False).head(10)

plt.figure(figsize=(12, 7))
sns.barplot(x='LN_IC50', y='DRUG_NAME', data=least_potent_drugs, hue='DRUG_NAME', palette='viridis', legend=False)
plt.title('Top 10 Least Potent Drugs by Average LN_IC50 (Higher is Less Potent)')
plt.xlabel('Average LN_IC50')
plt.ylabel('Drug Name')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Visualizing Top 10 Least Effective Drugs by AUC¶

Let's visualize the top 10 drugs with the highest average AUC values, indicating they are the least effective overall. A bar plot will effectively highlight these drugs and their relative effectiveness.

In [25]:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns

sns.set_style("whitegrid")

# Get the top 10 drugs by highest average AUC (least effective overall)
least_effective_drugs = drug_effectiveness.sort_values(by='AUC', ascending=False).head(10)

plt.figure(figsize=(12, 7))
sns.barplot(x='AUC', y='DRUG_NAME', data=least_effective_drugs, hue='DRUG_NAME', palette='magma', legend=False)
plt.title('Top 10 Least Effective Drugs by Average AUC (Higher is Less Effective)')
plt.xlabel('Average AUC')
plt.ylabel('Drug Name')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Visualizing Top 10 Least Sensitive Drugs by Z_SCORE¶

Let's visualize the top 10 drugs with the highest average Z_SCORE values, indicating they are the least sensitive (or most resistant) overall. A bar plot will effectively highlight these drugs and their relative sensitivity.

In [26]:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns

sns.set_style("whitegrid")

# Get the top 10 drugs by highest average Z_SCORE (least sensitive/most resistant)
least_sensitive_drugs = drug_effectiveness.sort_values(by='Z_SCORE', ascending=False).head(10)

plt.figure(figsize=(12, 7))
sns.barplot(x='Z_SCORE', y='DRUG_NAME', data=least_sensitive_drugs, hue='DRUG_NAME', palette='cividis', legend=False)
plt.title('Top 10 Least Sensitive Drugs by Average Z_SCORE (Higher is Less Sensitive)')
plt.xlabel('Average Z_SCORE')
plt.ylabel('Drug Name')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Identifying the Top 10 Drugs that are Considered Least Effective Overall¶

In [27]:
# Find the top 10 drugs by Highest Average LN_IC50 (least potent)
print("Top 10 Drugs by Highest Average LN_IC50 (least potent):")
display(drug_effectiveness.sort_values(by='LN_IC50', ascending=False).head(10))

# Find the top 10 drugs by Highest Average AUC (least effective overall)
print("\nTop 10 Drugs by Highest Average AUC (least effective overall):")
display(drug_effectiveness.sort_values(by='AUC', ascending=False).head(10))

# Find the top 10 drugs by Highest Average Z_SCORE (lowest sensitivity/highest resistance)
print("\nTop 10 Drugs by Highest Average Z_SCORE (lowest sensitivity/highest resistance):")
display(drug_effectiveness.sort_values(by='Z_SCORE', ascending=False).head(10))

Top 10 Drugs by Highest Average LN_IC50 (least potent):
DRUG_NAME LN_IC50 AUC Z_SCORE
244 ascorbate (vitamin C) 10.291935 0.963827 0.032547

245 glutathione 9.181100 0.969636 0.017659

Top 10 Drugs by Highest Average AUC (least effective overall):
DRUG_NAME LN_IC50 AUC Z_SCORE

111 IOX2 2.372007 0.981285 0.040874
92 GSK2256098C 4.353509 0.980234 0.070989

DRUG_NAME LN_IC50 AUC Z_SCORE
153 Niraparib 4.263269 0.942962 0.100720

166 PD173074 3.885804 0.941337 0.090486

Comments

Considering the top 10 drugs for each 'least effective' metric, no drug consistently appears in all three categories (highest average LN_IC50, highest average AUC, and highest average Z_SCORE).

Thus, different drugs exhibit their 'least effectiveness' based on the specific metric being considered.

Identifying Overlapping Least Effective Drugs¶

In [28]:
# Get the top 10 drugs for each metric
top_10_highest_ln_ic50 = set(drug_effectiveness.sort_values(by='LN_IC50', ascending=False).head(10)['DRUG_NAME'])
top_10_highest_auc = set(drug_effectiveness.sort_values(by='AUC', ascending=False).head(10)['DRUG_NAME'])
top_10_highest_z_score = set(drug_effectiveness.sort_values(by='Z_SCORE', ascending=False).head(10)['DRUG_NAME'])

# Find the intersection of these sets
overlapping_least_effective_drugs = top_10_highest_ln_ic50.intersection(top_10_highest_auc, top_10_highest_z_score)

print("Drugs appearing in the top 10 across all three 'least effective' metrics:")
if overlapping_least_effective_drugs:
    for drug in overlapping_least_effective_drugs:
        print(f"- {drug}")
else:
    print("No drugs found that are in the top 10 for all three 'least effective' metrics.")

Drugs appearing in the top 10 across all three 'least effective' metrics:
No drugs found that are in the top 10 for all three 'least effective' metrics.

Identifying Drugs Least Effective Across At Least Two Metrics¶

Since no drug appeared in the top 10 for all three 'least effective' metrics, let's broaden our search to identify drugs that are consistently least effective across at least two of the three metrics: LN_IC50, AUC, and Z_SCORE. This will help highlight drugs with broader ineffectiveness profiles.

In [29]:
import collections

# Calculate intersections for each pair of metrics
overlap_highest_ln_ic50_auc = top_10_highest_ln_ic50.intersection(top_10_highest_auc)
overlap_highest_ln_ic50_z_score = top_10_highest_ln_ic50.intersection(top_10_highest_z_score)
overlap_highest_auc_z_score = top_10_highest_auc.intersection(top_10_highest_z_score)

# Combine all drugs that appear in at least two metrics using a union
drugs_in_at_least_two_least_effective_metrics = overlap_highest_ln_ic50_auc.union(overlap_highest_ln_ic50_z_score, overlap_highest_auc_z_score)

print("Drugs appearing in the top 10 for at least two 'least effective' metrics:")
if drugs_in_at_least_two_least_effective_metrics:
    # Create a dictionary to store which metrics each drug is in the top 10 for
    drug_metric_breakdown_least = collections.defaultdict(list)
    for drug in drugs_in_at_least_two_least_effective_metrics:
        if drug in top_10_highest_ln_ic50:
            drug_metric_breakdown_least[drug].append('Highest_LN_IC50')
        if drug in top_10_highest_auc:
            drug_metric_breakdown_least[drug].append('Highest_AUC')
        if drug in top_10_highest_z_score:
            drug_metric_breakdown_least[drug].append('Highest_Z_SCORE')

    # Print the drugs along with their corresponding metrics
    for drug in sorted(drug_metric_breakdown_least.keys()):
        metrics = ', '.join(drug_metric_breakdown_least[drug])
        print(f"- {drug} ({metrics})")
else:
    print("No drugs found that are in the top 10 for at least two 'least effective' metrics.")

Drugs appearing in the top 10 for at least two 'least effective' metrics:
- EPZ5676 (Highest_LN_IC50, Highest_AUC)
- GSK2830371 (Highest_LN_IC50, Highest_AUC)
- LMB_AB1 (Highest_LN_IC50, Highest_AUC)
- Motesanib (Highest_LN_IC50, Highest_AUC)

Comments

The four drugs are consistently among the least effective, indicating that they have high IC50 values (low potency) and high AUC values (poor overall effectiveness).

This suggests that these drugs are generally less effective across the cell lines studied, based on these two important metrics.

Identifying the Single Least Effective Drug Overall¶

Among the drugs consistently identified as least effective across multiple metrics, let's pinpoint the single drug that is least effective overall. We will do this by comparing their average AUC values, where a higher AUC indicates lower overall effectiveness.

In [30]:
least_effective_overlap_drugs = ['EPZ5676', 'GSK2830371', 'LMB_AB1', 'Motesanib']

# Filter the drug_effectiveness DataFrame for these drugs
overall_least_effective_drugs_df = drug_effectiveness[drug_effectiveness['DRUG_NAME'].isin(least_effective_overlap_drugs)]

# Sort by AUC in descending order to find the least effective overall
overall_least_effective_drugs_df = overall_least_effective_drugs_df.sort_values(by='AUC', ascending=False)

print("Overall Least Effective Drugs (ranked by AUC):")
display(overall_least_effective_drugs_df)

Overall Least Effective Drugs (ranked by AUC):
DRUG_NAME LN_IC50 AUC Z_SCORE
74 EPZ5676 5.579809 0.980019 0.045079
147 Motesanib 5.555627 0.978912 0.075317
126 LMB_AB1 5.460292 0.978679 0.065686
97 GSK2830371 5.488680 0.977324 0.065695

Comments

Based on the average AUC values, EPZ5676 is identified as the single least effective drug among the four, with the highest AUC value of 0.980019.

It is followed by Motesanib, LMB_AB1, and GSK2830371.

Identifying Drugs with Highly Variable Responses¶

To identify drugs that show highly variable responses across different cell lines, we will calculate the standard deviation of LN_IC50, AUC, and Z_SCORE for each drug. A higher standard deviation indicates greater variability in response.

In [31]:
# Group by DRUG_NAME and calculate the standard deviation for the sensitivity metrics
drug_variability = gdsc.groupby('DRUG_NAME')[['LN_IC50', 'AUC', 'Z_SCORE']].std().reset_index()

print("Top 10 Drugs by Highest Standard Deviation of LN_IC50 (most variable potency):")
display(drug_variability.sort_values(by='LN_IC50', ascending=False).head(10))

print("\nTop 10 Drugs by Highest Standard Deviation of AUC (most variable effectiveness):")
display(drug_variability.sort_values(by='AUC', ascending=False).head(10))

print("\nTop 10 Drugs by Highest Standard Deviation of Z_SCORE (most variable sensitivity):")
display(drug_variability.sort_values(by='Z_SCORE', ascending=False).head(10))

Top 10 Drugs by Highest Standard Deviation of LN_IC50 (most variable potency):
DRUG_NAME LN_IC50 AUC Z_SCORE
105 Gemcitabine 2.941950 0.234819 0.977581
20 AZD5991 2.759418 0.186487 0.986386
67 Daporinad 2.735583 0.223516 0.987256
70 Docetaxel 2.546911 0.258092 0.992675
36 BI-2536 2.459347 0.198379 1.010322
68 Dasatinib 2.394058 0.172630 1.031775
144 Methotrexate 2.389344 0.172257 0.960298
217 Tozasertib 2.386865 0.185867 1.002931
19 AZD5582 2.345364 0.186384 1.004422
218 Trametinib 2.301612 0.194131 1.020231 
Top 10 Drugs by Highest Standard Deviation of AUC (most variable effectiveness):
DRUG_NAME LN_IC50 AUC Z_SCORE
65 Dactinomycin 1.860500 0.315533 0.991142
195 SN-38 1.933588 0.263135 0.953998
70 Docetaxel 2.546911 0.258092 0.992675
105 Gemcitabine 2.941950 0.234819 0.977581
67 Daporinad 2.735583 0.223516 0.987256
51 CDK9_5038 1.940858 0.201548 1.006713
36 BI-2536 2.459347 0.198379 1.010322
218 Trametinib 2.301612 0.194131 1.020231
128 LMP744 2.279247 0.191312 0.978920
28 Alisertib 2.267331 0.187232 0.991223 
Top 10 Drugs by Highest Standard Deviation of Z_SCORE (most variable sensitivity):
DRUG_NAME LN_IC50 AUC Z_SCORE
46 Bosutinib 1.597128 0.088471 1.045139
26 Afatinib 1.523329 0.120106 1.038790
189 SB505124 1.576166 0.073126 1.038549
243 alpha-lipoic acid 1.002309 0.016634 1.037650
235 WEHI-539 1.777534 0.118783 1.036918
155 OF-1 1.208928 0.043207 1.036505
160 Osimertinib 1.321848 0.068677 1.035487
115 Ipatasertib 1.503115 0.088412 1.034462
17 AZD5363 1.488519 0.098497 1.033524
63 Dabrafenib 1.909821 0.123146 1.032732

Comments

These tables highlight drugs for which the response metrics (LN_IC50, AUC, Z_SCORE) vary significantly across different cell lines. This high variability suggests that these drugs might be very effective in some cancer contexts but much less so in others, indicating a need for careful selection based on specific cell line or cancer type characteristics.

Identifying Overlapping Highly Variable Drugs¶

To find drugs that are consistently highly variable across all three metrics (LN_IC50, AUC, and Z_SCORE), we'll identify the drugs that appear in the top 10 for the highest standard deviation of each of these metrics and then find their intersection.

In [32]:
# Get the top 10 drugs for each metric by highest standard deviation
top_10_sd_ln_ic50 = set(drug_variability.sort_values(by='LN_IC50', ascending=False).head(10)['DRUG_NAME'])
top_10_sd_auc = set(drug_variability.sort_values(by='AUC', ascending=False).head(10)['DRUG_NAME'])
top_10_sd_z_score = set(drug_variability.sort_values(by='Z_SCORE', ascending=False).head(10)['DRUG_NAME'])

# Find the intersection of these sets
overlapping_highly_variable_drugs_all = top_10_sd_ln_ic50.intersection(top_10_sd_auc, top_10_sd_z_score)

print("Drugs appearing in the top 10 for highest standard deviation across all three variability metrics:")
if overlapping_highly_variable_drugs_all:
    for drug in overlapping_highly_variable_drugs_all:
        print(f"- {drug}")
else:
    print("No drugs found that are in the top 10 for highest standard deviation across all three metrics.")

Drugs appearing in the top 10 for highest standard deviation across all three variability metrics:
No drugs found that are in the top 10 for highest standard deviation across all three metrics.

Identifying Drugs Highly Variable Across At Least Two Metrics¶

Since no drug appeared in the top 10 for all three variability metrics, let's broaden our search to identify drugs that are consistently highly variable across at least two of the three metrics: LN_IC50, AUC, and Z_SCORE. This will help highlight drugs with broader variability profiles.

In [33]:
import collections

# Calculate intersections for each pair of metrics
overlap_sd_ln_ic50_auc = top_10_sd_ln_ic50.intersection(top_10_sd_auc)
overlap_sd_ln_ic50_z_score = top_10_sd_ln_ic50.intersection(top_10_sd_z_score)
overlap_sd_auc_z_score = top_10_sd_auc.intersection(top_10_sd_z_score)

# Combine all drugs that appear in at least two metrics using a union
drugs_in_at_least_two_variable_metrics = overlap_sd_ln_ic50_auc.union(overlap_sd_ln_ic50_z_score, overlap_sd_auc_z_score)

print("Drugs appearing in the top 10 for highest standard deviation across at least two variability metrics:")
if drugs_in_at_least_two_variable_metrics:
    # Create a dictionary to store which metrics each drug is in the top 10 for
    drug_metric_breakdown_variable = collections.defaultdict(list)
    for drug in drugs_in_at_least_two_variable_metrics:
        if drug in top_10_sd_ln_ic50:
            drug_metric_breakdown_variable[drug].append('Highest_SD_LN_IC50')
        if drug in top_10_sd_auc:
            drug_metric_breakdown_variable[drug].append('Highest_SD_AUC')
        if drug in top_10_sd_z_score:
            drug_metric_breakdown_variable[drug].append('Highest_SD_Z_SCORE')

    # Print the drugs along with their corresponding metrics
    for drug in sorted(drug_metric_breakdown_variable.keys()):
        metrics = ', '.join(drug_metric_breakdown_variable[drug])
        print(f"- {drug} ({metrics})")
else:
    print("No drugs found that are in the top 10 for highest standard deviation across at least two metrics.")

Drugs appearing in the top 10 for highest standard deviation across at least two variability metrics:
- BI-2536 (Highest_SD_LN_IC50, Highest_SD_AUC)
- Daporinad (Highest_SD_LN_IC50, Highest_SD_AUC)
- Docetaxel (Highest_SD_LN_IC50, Highest_SD_AUC)
- Gemcitabine (Highest_SD_LN_IC50, Highest_SD_AUC)
- Trametinib (Highest_SD_LN_IC50, Highest_SD_AUC)

Identifying the Single Most Variable Drug Overall¶

Among the drugs consistently identified as highly variable across multiple metrics, let's pinpoint the single drug that is most variable overall. We will do this by comparing a combined score of their average standard deviations for LN_IC50 and AUC.

In [34]:
highly_variable_overlap_drugs = ['BI-2536', 'Daporinad', 'Docetaxel', 'Gemcitabine', 'Trametinib']

# Filter the drug_variability DataFrame for these drugs
overall_most_variable_drugs_df = drug_variability[drug_variability['DRUG_NAME'].isin(highly_variable_overlap_drugs)].copy()

# Calculate a combined variability score (e.g., sum of std for LN_IC50 and AUC)
# Since they were identified based on these two metrics, summing them makes sense.
overall_most_variable_drugs_df['Combined_Variability_Score'] = \
    overall_most_variable_drugs_df['LN_IC50'] + overall_most_variable_drugs_df['AUC']

# Sort by Combined_Variability_Score in descending order to find the most variable overall
overall_most_variable_drugs_df = overall_most_variable_drugs_df.sort_values(by='Combined_Variability_Score', ascending=False)

print("Overall Most Variable Drugs (ranked by Combined Variability Score - LN_IC50 + AUC):")
display(overall_most_variable_drugs_df)

Overall Most Variable Drugs (ranked by Combined Variability Score - LN_IC50 + AUC):
DRUG_NAME LN_IC50 AUC Z_SCORE Combined_Variability_Score
105 Gemcitabine 2.941950 0.234819 0.977581 3.176768
67 Daporinad 2.735583 0.223516 0.987256 2.959099
70 Docetaxel 2.546911 0.258092 0.992675 2.805003
36 BI-2536 2.459347 0.198379 1.010322 2.657726
218 Trametinib 2.301612 0.194131 1.020231 2.495743

Comments

The table above identifies the overall most variable drug among the selected five, based on a combined score of their standard deviations for LN_IC50 and AUC.

The drug with the highest 'Combined_Variability_Score' is considered the most variable, indicating the broadest range of responses across different cell lines for these two key metrics.

This means that Gemcitabine exhibits the broadest range of responses across different cell lines concerning its potency (LN_IC50) and overall effectiveness (AUC).

This high variability suggests that its effectiveness can differ significantly depending on the specific cellular context.

Cancer Cell Line Analysis¶

Identifying Best Performing Drugs Per Cancer Type¶

To identify drugs that perform best in specific cancer types, we will analyze the cancer_type_drug_effectiveness DataFrame. For each cancer type (TCGA_DESC), we will find the drug that exhibits the lowest average LN_IC50, lowest average AUC, and most negative average Z_SCORE.

In [35]:
cancer_type_drug_effectiveness = gdsc.groupby(['TCGA_DESC', 'DRUG_NAME'])[['LN_IC50', 'AUC', 'Z_SCORE']].mean().reset_index()

# Find the drug with the lowest LN_IC50 for each cancer type (most potent)
best_ln_ic50_per_cancer = cancer_type_drug_effectiveness.loc[cancer_type_drug_effectiveness.groupby('TCGA_DESC')['LN_IC50'].idxmin()]
print("Best Drug (by Lowest LN_IC50) per Cancer Type:")
display(best_ln_ic50_per_cancer.sort_values(by='TCGA_DESC'))

# Find the drug with the lowest AUC for each cancer type (most effective overall)
best_auc_per_cancer = cancer_type_drug_effectiveness.loc[cancer_type_drug_effectiveness.groupby('TCGA_DESC')['AUC'].idxmin()]
print("\nBest Drug (by Lowest AUC) per Cancer Type:")
display(best_auc_per_cancer.sort_values(by='TCGA_DESC'))

# Find the drug with the most negative Z_SCORE for each cancer type (highest sensitivity)
best_z_score_per_cancer = cancer_type_drug_effectiveness.loc[cancer_type_drug_effectiveness.groupby('TCGA_DESC')['Z_SCORE'].idxmin()]
print("\nBest Drug (by Most Negative Z_SCORE) per Cancer Type:")
display(best_z_score_per_cancer.sort_values(by='TCGA_DESC'))

Best Drug (by Lowest LN_IC50) per Cancer Type:

7070 UCEC Daporinad -5.600786 0.519559 -0.799654

Comments

'Sepantronium bromide' appears to be the most consistently effective drug across the widest range of cancer types when considering both potency (LN_IC50) and overall effectiveness (AUC and often Z_SCORE).

'Daporinad' also shows strong effectiveness, particularly in certain cancer types, often ranking highest in LN_IC50 and Z_SCORE for those types where Sepantronium bromide is not dominant.

'Dactinomycin' shows targeted effectiveness in a smaller subset of cancer types.

This detailed breakdown allows us to identify specific drug-cancer type combinations where particular drugs show optimal performance.

Drug Effectiveness by TARGET_PATHWAY¶

To understand how drug effectiveness relates to the TARGET_PATHWAY, we will group the data by this categorical feature and calculate the average LN_IC50, AUC, and Z_SCORE. This will help identify pathways where drugs tend to be more or less effective.

In [36]:
# Group by TARGET_PATHWAY and calculate the mean for the sensitivity metrics
pathway_effectiveness = gdsc.groupby('TARGET_PATHWAY')[['LN_IC50', 'AUC', 'Z_SCORE']].mean().reset_index()

print("Top 10 TARGET_PATHWAYS by Lowest Average LN_IC50 (most potent drugs targeting these pathways):")
display(pathway_effectiveness.sort_values(by='LN_IC50').head(10))

print("\nTop 10 TARGET_PATHWAYS by Lowest Average AUC (most effective drugs targeting these pathways):")
display(pathway_effectiveness.sort_values(by='AUC').head(10))

print("\nTop 10 TARGET_PATHWAYS by Most Negative Average Z_SCORE (highest sensitivity for drugs targeting these pathways):")
display(pathway_effectiveness.sort_values(by='Z_SCORE').head(10))

Top 10 TARGET_PATHWAYS by Lowest Average LN_IC50 (most potent drugs targeting these pathways):
TARGET_PATHWAY LN_IC50 AUC Z_SCORE
15 Mitosis -1.397001 0.780781 0.046195
19 Protein stability and degradation 0.831953 0.816259 0.014285
2 Cell cycle 1.544684 0.791395 0.049025
1 Apoptosis regulation 1.777141 0.772716 0.035750
7 DNA replication 2.098566 0.848893 0.060296
18 PI3K/MTOR signaling 2.565856 0.881721 0.028881
20 RTK signaling 2.588115 0.862163 0.052273
8 EGFR signaling 2.631116 0.916830 -0.018903
3 Chromatin histone acetylation 2.680639 0.909610 0.048109
9 ERK MAPK signaling 2.893182 0.887183 -0.023232 
Top 10 TARGET_PATHWAYS by Lowest Average AUC (most effective drugs targeting these pathways):
TARGET_PATHWAY LN_IC50 AUC Z_SCORE
1 Apoptosis regulation 1.777141 0.772716 0.035750
15 Mitosis -1.397001 0.780781 0.046195
2 Cell cycle 1.544684 0.791395 0.049025
19 Protein stability and degradation 0.831953 0.816259 0.014285
7 DNA replication 2.098566 0.848893 0.060296
20 RTK signaling 2.588115 0.862163 0.052273
12 IGF1R signaling 2.999228 0.873448 0.057792
18 PI3K/MTOR signaling 2.565856 0.881721 0.028881
6 Cytoskeleton 3.215344 0.884841 0.045165
9 ERK MAPK signaling 2.893182 0.887183 -0.023232 
Top 10 TARGET_PATHWAYS by Most Negative Average Z_SCORE (highest sensitivity for drugs targeting these pathways):
TARGET_PATHWAY LN_IC50 AUC Z_SCORE
9 ERK MAPK signaling 2.893182 0.887183 -0.023232
8 EGFR signaling 2.631116 0.916830 -0.018903
0 ABL signaling 3.124303 0.935846 0.011229
19 Protein stability and degradation 0.831953 0.816259 0.014285
21 WNT signaling 4.029785 0.947325 0.019455
18 PI3K/MTOR signaling 2.565856 0.881721 0.028881
14 Metabolism 4.807937 0.918521 0.031013
11 Hormone-related 4.213764 0.962813 0.031182
1 Apoptosis regulation 1.777141 0.772716 0.035750
16 Other 3.581629 0.887623 0.042965

Visualizing Drug Effectiveness Across TARGET_PATHWAYS¶

To visually summarize the drug effectiveness across different target pathways, we will use bar plots for LN_IC50, AUC, and Z_SCORE. These plots will help in quickly identifying pathways that are generally associated with higher or lower drug sensitivity.

In [37]:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns

sns.set_style("whitegrid")

# Sort pathways by LN_IC50 for consistent ordering in plots
pathway_effectiveness_sorted = pathway_effectiveness.sort_values(by='LN_IC50', ascending=True)

plt.figure(figsize=(15, 18))

# Bar plot for LN_IC50
plt.subplot(3, 1, 1) # 3 rows, 1 column, 1st plot
sns.barplot(x='LN_IC50', y='TARGET_PATHWAY', data=pathway_effectiveness_sorted, hue='TARGET_PATHWAY', palette='viridis', legend=False)
plt.title('Average LN_IC50 by TARGET_PATHWAY (Lower is Better)')
plt.xlabel('Average LN_IC50')
plt.ylabel('TARGET_PATHWAY')

# Bar plot for AUC
plt.subplot(3, 1, 2) # 3 rows, 1 column, 2nd plot
sns.barplot(x='AUC', y='TARGET_PATHWAY', data=pathway_effectiveness_sorted, hue='TARGET_PATHWAY', palette='magma', legend=False)
plt.title('Average AUC by TARGET_PATHWAY (Lower is Better)')
plt.xlabel('Average AUC')
plt.ylabel('TARGET_PATHWAY')

# Bar plot for Z_SCORE
plt.subplot(3, 1, 3) # 3 rows, 1 column, 3rd plot
sns.barplot(x='Z_SCORE', y='TARGET_PATHWAY', data=pathway_effectiveness_sorted, hue='TARGET_PATHWAY', palette='cividis', legend=False)
plt.title('Average Z_SCORE by TARGET_PATHWAY (More Negative is Better)')
plt.xlabel('Average Z_SCORE')
plt.ylabel('TARGET_PATHWAY')

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Comments:

Based on the analysis of drug effectiveness across different TARGET_PATHWAYs, here's a summary of the key findings:

Most Effective Pathways: Pathways such as 'Mitosis', 'Apoptosis regulation', and 'ERK MAPK signaling' consistently show the lowest average LN_IC50 (highest potency), lowest average AUC (highest overall effectiveness), and most negative average Z_SCORE (highest sensitivity). This suggests that drugs targeting these pathways tend to be broadly effective.

Consistently Least Effective Pathways: JNK and p38 signaling consistently appears as one of the least effective pathways across all three metrics. p53 pathway also shows up in the top (least effective) ranks for LN_IC50 and Z_SCORE.

In essence, the 'Mitosis', 'Apoptosis regulation', and 'EPK MAPK signaling' pathways emerge as promising targets for drug development due to the high effectiveness of drugs that modulate them, while other pathways show more resistance or less pronounced drug responses.

Influence of Genomic Features (CNA, Gene Expression, Methylation) on Drug Sensitivity¶

To determine if genomic features such as CNA (Copy Number Alteration), Gene Expression, and Methylation influence drug sensitivity, we will analyze the relationship between these categorical variables and the drug sensitivity metrics (LN_IC50, AUC, Z_SCORE).

For each genomic feature, we will:

  1. Calculate descriptive statistics (mean and standard deviation) of the sensitivity metrics for each category (e.g., 'Yes' vs. 'No').
  2. Perform an independent samples t-test to check for statistically significant differences in the means of the sensitivity metrics between the categories.
  3. Visualize the distributions using box plots to illustrate the differences.
In [38]:
from scipy.stats import ttest_ind
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns

genomic_features = ['CNA', 'Gene Expression', 'Methylation']
sensitivity_metrics = ['LN_IC50', 'AUC', 'Z_SCORE']

sns.set_style("whitegrid")

for feature in genomic_features:
    print(f"\n--- Analysis for {feature} ---")

    # Check unique values to ensure it's a binary categorical feature (e.g., 'Y', 'N')
    unique_values = gdsc[feature].unique()
    if len(unique_values) != 2:
        print(f"Skipping {feature}: Not a binary categorical feature with 2 unique values. Found: {unique_values}")
        continue

    group1_name, group2_name = unique_values[0], unique_values[1]

    # Descriptive Statistics
    print(f"Descriptive Statistics of Sensitivity Metrics by {feature}:")
    display(gdsc.groupby(feature)[sensitivity_metrics].agg(['mean', 'std']))

    # Statistical Test (Independent Samples T-test)
    print(f"\nIndependent Samples T-test Results for {feature}:")
    for metric in sensitivity_metrics:
        group1_data = gdsc[gdsc[feature] == group1_name][metric]
        group2_data = gdsc[gdsc[feature] == group2_name][metric]

        # Handle cases where one group might be empty or have too few observations for t-test
        if len(group1_data) < 2 or len(group2_data) < 2:
            print(f"  Skipping t-test for {metric} due to insufficient data in one or both groups.")
            continue

        t_stat, p_val = ttest_ind(group1_data, group2_data, equal_var=False) # Welch's t-test
        print(f"  {metric}: t-statistic = {t_stat:.3f}, p-value = {p_val:.3f}")
        if p_val < 0.05:
            print(f"    There is a significant difference in {metric} between {group1_name} and {group2_name} groups (p < 0.05).")
        else:
            print(f"    No significant difference in {metric} between {group1_name} and {group2_name} groups (p >= 0.05).")

    # Visualization (Box Plots)
    plt.figure(figsize=(15, 5))
    for i, metric in enumerate(sensitivity_metrics):
        plt.subplot(1, 3, i + 1)
        sns.boxplot(x=feature, y=metric, data=gdsc, hue=feature, palette='pastel', legend=False)
        plt.title(f'Distribution of {metric} by {feature}')
        plt.xlabel(feature)
        plt.ylabel(metric)
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()

--- Analysis for CNA ---
Descriptive Statistics of Sensitivity Metrics by CNA:
LN_IC50 AUC Z_SCORE
mean std mean std mean std
CNA
N 3.128990 2.503630 0.894110 0.115493 0.249073 0.762390
Y 2.821395 2.837446 0.879866 0.148709 0.036850 0.999858 
Independent Samples T-test Results for CNA:
  LN_IC50: t-statistic = -3.143, p-value = 0.002
    There is a significant difference in LN_IC50 between Y and N groups (p < 0.05).
  AUC: t-statistic = -3.153, p-value = 0.002
    There is a significant difference in AUC between Y and N groups (p < 0.05).
  Z_SCORE: t-statistic = -7.116, p-value = 0.000
    There is a significant difference in Z_SCORE between Y and N groups (p < 0.05).

--- Analysis for Gene Expression ---
Descriptive Statistics of Sensitivity Metrics by Gene Expression:
LN_IC50 AUC Z_SCORE
mean std mean std mean std
Gene Expression
N 2.377815 2.703048 0.859263 0.166653 -0.223387 1.023152
Y 2.833210 2.838479 0.880415 0.148102 0.043913 0.997692 
Independent Samples T-test Results for Gene Expression:
  LN_IC50: t-statistic = 10.199, p-value = 0.000
    There is a significant difference in LN_IC50 between Y and N groups (p < 0.05).
  AUC: t-statistic = 7.712, p-value = 0.000
    There is a significant difference in AUC between Y and N groups (p < 0.05).
  Z_SCORE: t-statistic = 15.844, p-value = 0.000
    There is a significant difference in Z_SCORE between Y and N groups (p < 0.05).

--- Analysis for Methylation ---
Descriptive Statistics of Sensitivity Metrics by Methylation:
LN_IC50 AUC Z_SCORE
mean std mean std mean std
Methylation
N 2.796813 2.818811 0.873073 0.152076 0.048304 1.053684
Y 2.823214 2.836621 0.880075 0.148511 0.037478 0.997861 
Independent Samples T-test Results for Methylation:
  LN_IC50: t-statistic = 0.548, p-value = 0.584
    No significant difference in LN_IC50 between Y and N groups (p >= 0.05).
  AUC: t-statistic = 2.697, p-value = 0.007
    There is a significant difference in AUC between Y and N groups (p < 0.05).
  Z_SCORE: t-statistic = -0.602, p-value = 0.547
    No significant difference in Z_SCORE between Y and N groups (p >= 0.05).

Comments:

The presence of Copy Number Alteration ('Y') is significantly associated with increased drug potency (lower LN_IC50), greater overall effectiveness (lower AUC), and higher sensitivity (more negative Z_SCORE).

The presence of Gene Expression (Y) is significantly associated with lower drug potency (higher LN_IC50), lower overall effectiveness (higher AUC), and lower sensitivity (more negative Z_SCORE).

Methylation is significantly associated with lower effectiveness (higher AUC).

Multivariate Regression Analysis: Genomic Features vs. Drug Metrics¶

To better quantify the influence of CNA, Gene Expression, and Methylation on drug sensitivity, we will perform a multivariate regression analysis. We will model each drug sensitivity metric (LN_IC50, AUC, Z_SCORE) as a dependent variable, and the numerical representations of the genomic features as independent variables. This will allow us to assess the statistical significance and magnitude of the effect of each genomic feature, while controlling for others.

In [39]:
import statsmodels.formula.api as smf

# Create a copy of the gdsc DataFrame for correlation analysis and add numerical genomic features
df_corr = gdsc.copy()
df_corr['CNA_num'] = df_corr['CNA'].map({'Y': 1, 'N': 0})
df_corr['Gene_Expression_num'] = df_corr['Gene Expression'].map({'Y': 1, 'N': 0})
df_corr['Methylation_num'] = df_corr['Methylation'].map({'Y': 1, 'N': 0})

# Regression for LN_IC50
model_ln_ic50 = smf.ols('LN_IC50 ~ CNA_num + Gene_Expression_num + Methylation_num', data=df_corr).fit()
print("\nRegression Results for LN_IC50 (Lower is Better):\n")
print(model_ln_ic50.summary())

Regression Results for LN_IC50 (Lower is Better):

                            OLS Regression Results                            
==============================================================================
Dep. Variable:                LN_IC50   R-squared:                       0.001
Model:                            OLS   Adj. R-squared:                  0.001
Method:                 Least Squares   F-statistic:                     36.63
Date:                Thu, 07 May 2026   Prob (F-statistic):           1.18e-23
Time:                        03:58:38   Log-Likelihood:            -3.9895e+05
No. Observations:              162103   AIC:                         7.979e+05
Df Residuals:                  162099   BIC:                         7.979e+05
Df Model:                           3                                         
Covariance Type:            nonrobust                                         
=======================================================================================
                          coef    std err          t      P>|t|      [0.025      0.975]
---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Intercept               2.7735      0.125     22.181      0.000       2.528       3.019
CNA_num                -0.2991      0.111     -2.700      0.007      -0.516      -0.082
Gene_Expression_num     0.4998      0.050     10.095      0.000       0.403       0.597
Methylation_num        -0.1443      0.051     -2.815      0.005      -0.245      -0.044
==============================================================================
Omnibus:                    16330.937   Durbin-Watson:                   0.486
Prob(Omnibus):                  0.000   Jarque-Bera (JB):            23323.682
Skew:                          -0.792   Prob(JB):                         0.00
Kurtosis:                       3.972   Cond. No.                         45.8
==============================================================================

Notes:
[1] Standard Errors assume that the covariance matrix of the errors is correctly specified.

Interpretation for LN_IC50 Regression:¶

R-squared (0.001): This value is very low, indicating that only 0.1% of the variability in LN_IC50 can be explained by the three genomic features (CNA_num, Gene_Expression_num, Methylation_num) combined. This suggests that while these features might have a statistically significant impact, their practical effect on drug potency, when considered together in this linear model, is quite small.

Prob (F-statistic) (1.18e-23): This extremely low p-value (much less than 0.05) indicates that the overall regression model is statistically significant. This means that at least one of the genomic features is significantly related to LN_IC50.

Coefficients and P-values for individual features:

CNA_num (coef = -0.2991, P>|t| = 0.007): The negative coefficient and significant p-value (0.007 < 0.05) suggest that the presence of Copy Number Alteration (CNA) is associated with a decrease in LN_IC50. This implies that CNA is associated with higher drug potency, controlling for the other genomic features.

Gene_Expression_num (coef = 0.4998, P>|t| = 0.000): The positive coefficient and highly significant p-value (0.000 < 0.05) indicate that the presence of Gene Expression is associated with an increase in LN_IC50. This means Gene Expression is associated with lower drug potency.

Methylation_num (coef = -0.1443, P>|t| = 0.005): The negative coefficient and significant p-value (0.005 < 0.05) suggest that the presence of Methylation is associated with a decrease in LN_IC50. This implies that Methylation is associated with higher drug potency.

These results align with the t-test findings for CNA and Gene Expression, suggesting that CNA presence is generally associated with increased potency, while gene expression is associated with decreased potency. The significance for Methylation might differ due to controlling for other variables in the regression model.

In [40]:
# Regression for AUC
model_auc = smf.ols('AUC ~ CNA_num + Gene_Expression_num + Methylation_num', data=df_corr).fit()
print("\nRegression Results for AUC (Lower is Better):\n")
print(model_auc.summary())

Regression Results for AUC (Lower is Better):

                            OLS Regression Results                            
==============================================================================
Dep. Variable:                    AUC   R-squared:                       0.000
Model:                            OLS   Adj. R-squared:                  0.000
Method:                 Least Squares   F-statistic:                     26.70
Date:                Thu, 07 May 2026   Prob (F-statistic):           2.95e-17
Time:                        03:59:13   Log-Likelihood:                 79084.
No. Observations:              162103   AIC:                        -1.582e+05
Df Residuals:                  162099   BIC:                        -1.581e+05
Df Model:                           3                                         
Covariance Type:            nonrobust                                         
=======================================================================================
                          coef    std err          t      P>|t|      [0.025      0.975]
---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Intercept               0.8732      0.007    133.280      0.000       0.860       0.886
CNA_num                -0.0138      0.006     -2.370      0.018      -0.025      -0.002
Gene_Expression_num     0.0212      0.003      8.160      0.000       0.016       0.026
Methylation_num        -0.0002      0.003     -0.086      0.931      -0.005       0.005
==============================================================================
Omnibus:                    79156.158   Durbin-Watson:                   0.763
Prob(Omnibus):                  0.000   Jarque-Bera (JB):           442093.236
Skew:                          -2.370   Prob(JB):                         0.00
Kurtosis:                       9.556   Cond. No.                         45.8
==============================================================================

Notes:
[1] Standard Errors assume that the covariance matrix of the errors is correctly specified.

Comments

In summary, the presence of Copy Number Alteration (CNA) is associated with improved overall drug effectiveness (lower AUC), while Gene Expression is associated with reduced overall effectiveness (higher AUC).

Methylation, however, does not show a significant linear relationship with AUC in this model. The overall explanatory power of these three genomic features for AUC is very limited.

In [41]:
# Regression for Z_SCORE
model_z_score = smf.ols('Z_SCORE ~ CNA_num + Gene_Expression_num + Methylation_num', data=df_corr).fit()
print("\nRegression Results for Z_SCORE (More Negative is Better):\n")
print(model_z_score.summary())

Regression Results for Z_SCORE (More Negative is Better):

                            OLS Regression Results                            
==============================================================================
Dep. Variable:                Z_SCORE   R-squared:                       0.002
Model:                            OLS   Adj. R-squared:                  0.002
Method:                 Least Squares   F-statistic:                     110.5
Date:                Thu, 07 May 2026   Prob (F-statistic):           1.78e-71
Time:                        04:13:32   Log-Likelihood:            -2.2970e+05
No. Observations:              162103   AIC:                         4.594e+05
Df Residuals:                  162099   BIC:                         4.595e+05
Df Model:                           3                                         
Covariance Type:            nonrobust                                         
=======================================================================================
                          coef    std err          t      P>|t|      [0.025      0.975]
---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Intercept               0.0608      0.044      1.381      0.167      -0.025       0.147
CNA_num                -0.2077      0.039     -5.326      0.000      -0.284      -0.131
Gene_Expression_num     0.3026      0.017     17.361      0.000       0.268       0.337
Methylation_num        -0.1143      0.018     -6.333      0.000      -0.150      -0.079
